function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
# Composition of AI Segment Revenue for FY2025 ($3,201 Million)

## Revenue Disaggregation by Type

Based on the revenue disaggregation table in the filing, the AI segment's $3,201 million is composed of two broad categories:

| Revenue Type | FY2025 ($ millions) |
|---|---|
| Advertising | $1,844 |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure | $1,357 |
| **Total AI Segment** | **$3,201** |

## Revenue Streams Included in the AI Segment

The filing describes the AI segment as generating revenue from **the sale of digital platform services, including advertising, subscription, and licensing services** offered to consumers and enterprise customers. Specifically, the segment comprises two principal revenue categories:

### 1. Advertising Revenue ($1,844M)
- Sale of ad products displayed on the **X platform**
- Recognized in the period when advertising is delivered, as evidenced by a user engaging with an ad in a manner satisfying the advertiser's selected engagement criteria
- The Company acts as principal for ads sold directly through X (gross recognition) and as agent for ads sold through SSP partners

### 2. AI Solutions & Infrastructure Revenue ($1,357M)
This category includes three sub-streams:

- **(i) Premium subscriptions on X and Grok** — recognized ratably over the subscription term (ranging from month-to-month to one year)
- **(ii) Data licensing arrangements** — generally recognized ratably over the period (from month-to-month to two years) as the customer consumes and benefits from the use of the licensed data

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 text, here are the answers to your questions:

**1. Grok Subscription Pricing, Paying Subscribers, and Free vs. Paid Breakdown**
Yes, the S-1 discloses details on all three aspects:
*   **Pricing:** The S-1 notes that Grok subscriptions are offered in tiers (basic, SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite) priced on a monthly or annual basis. In its market opportunity analysis, the company assumes a weighted average monthly subscription revenue of $12. It specifically assumes a $30 monthly cost for a SuperGrok subscription in high-income countries, $8 in upper-middle and lower-middle-income countries, and significantly lower costs in low-income countries.
*   **Number of Paying Grok Subscribers:** As of March 31, 2026, there were approximately 1.9 million paid subscribers across the SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite tiers. 
*   **Free vs. Paid Breakdown:** The S-1 states that as of March 31, 2026, there were approximately 117 million Monthly Active Users (MAUs) who used Grok’s AI features. By comparing this to the 1.9 million paid Grok subscribers, the breakdown shows that the vast majority of Grok users (over 115 million) are on the free tier. The company explicitly notes that it is "still early in increasing paid penetration across our Grok user base."

**2. Temporal Relationship Between MAU Figures and FY2025 Revenue Figures**
There is a three-month forward lag between the two metrics:
*   **MAU Figures:** These are point-in-time metrics measured "as of March 31, 2026" (representing the 30-day period ending on that date). 
*   **FY2025 Revenue Figures:** These are historical financial metrics covering the fiscal year "ended December 31, 2025."
*   **Temporal Relationship:** The MAU figures provide a more current snapshot of user engagement, captured exactly three months after the close of the fiscal year for which the FY2025 revenue figures are reported. 

**3. Details from the Specified S-1 Chunk**
The chunk outlining the company's monetization strategy details the following:
*   **AI-Driven Advertising Initiatives:** The company focuses on making ads feel like content by ensuring they are contextually relevant, aligned with user interests, and integrated into real-time conversations. Grok supports this by helping advertisers with campaign creation, creative optimization, and alignment with trending topics and user intent. While ad pricing is affected by the global economy and competition, the company believes continued investment in AI-powered advertising will improve advertiser ROI.
*   **Conversion to Paid Subscriptions:** The company is focused on converting its user base into paying subscribers through X (Premium and Premium+) and Grok subscription offerings. Subscribers gain enhanced functionality, exclusive features, and access to the latest AI models.
*   **Subscriber Metrics:** As of March 31, 2026, the company reached approximately 6.3 million active paid subscribers in total. This consisted of roughly 4.4 million X Premium and Premium+ paid subscribers and approximately 1.9 million SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite paid subscribers.
*   **Grok's Rapid Model Development:** The AI segment has demonstrated exceptional "model velocity," developing leading frontier models at a faster rate of innovation than competitors since launching Grok. The company plans to continue adding features and releasing increasingly capable Grok models to increase the subscriber penetration rate, believing this pace of innovation strengthens the subscription value proposition and supports long-term subscriber growth.

Based on the provided S-1 excerpts, here are the details regarding the AI segment, the relationship between X and Grok, and the specific revenue recognition policies:

### 1. Relationship Between X Platform Advertising Revenue and Grok
The S-1 describes a highly integrated, complementary relationship between X’s advertising business and Grok’s AI capabilities:
*   **Grok Enhances X Advertising:** Grok is embedded into X’s advertising strategy to help advertisers with "campaign creation, creative optimization, and alignment with trending topics and user intent." The new X Ads Manager (rolled out in April 2026) uses AI-powered systems for more precise ad delivery.
*   **X Enhances Grok:** X serves as a "foundational distribution and data engine" for Grok. The platform's real-time content stream and engagement feedback strengthen Grok’s ad product performance and relevance. 
*   **Current Limitations on Monetization:** Currently, Grok API access is **not** included in advertising rates, and the company does not currently sell or offer advertisers the ability to place ads via the Grok API. 
*   **Future Standalone Grok Ads:** The company notes that while ads are currently an X-centric monetization channel, there "might be an incremental monetization opportunity by introducing advertising into our stand-alone Grok offering" in the future.

### 2. Is the Advertising Revenue in the AI Segment Primarily from X or Grok?
The advertising revenue in the AI segment is **primarily from the X platform**. 
*   The S-1 explicitly states that the AI segment generates revenue from "(i) the sale of ad products displayed on its X platform, and (ii) providing AI solutions and infrastructure..."
*   Revenue is generated by monetizing "user activity through high-impact advertising inventory on X."
*   As noted above, advertising is not currently sold through the standalone Grok offering or via the Grok API.

### 3. How the AI Segment is Defined and Its Included Businesses
The AI segment is defined as generating revenue from the sale of digital platform services—including advertising, subscription, and licensing services—offered to consumers and enterprise customers. 

Specifically, the AI segment includes two main revenue categories:
1.  **Advertising:** The sale of ad products displayed on the X platform.
2.  **AI Solutions and Infrastructure:** This includes:
    *   **Subscription offerings:** Premium subscriptions on X (Premium and Premium+) and Grok subscriptions (basic, SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite).
    *   **Data licensing arrangements:** Providing continuous, high-volume, real-time data streams to customers.
    *   **API access to Grok models:** Providing developers and enterprises with API access to Grok models (including tailored deployments via Grok Business and Grok Enterprise).

### 4. Details from the Revenue Recognition Note
The provided chunk from the revenue recognition note outlines significant judgments for Starshield and the specific policies for the AI segment:

**Starshield Cost Estimation:**
*   Starshield contracts are complex, requiring the company to estimate total costs to perform over the contract term and measure progress toward completion for each performance obligation.
*   Developing these estimates requires **significant management judgment**, including assumptions about labor hours, allocation of shared satellite production costs, satellite material costs, and expected technological changes to satellites.
*   The company recognizes changes in estimated contract revenue or costs at completion as they occur.

**AI Segment Advertising Revenue Policy:**
*   Advertising revenue is recognized in the period the advertising is delivered, which is evidenced by a user engaging with the ad in a manner that satisfies the advertiser’s selected engagement criteria.
*   **Principal vs. Agent Evaluation:** The company evaluates whether it acts as principal or agent when third parties are involved.
    *   *Direct X Platform Sales:* For ads sold directly through the X platform, the company controls the ad services prior to transfer, is responsible for serving the ads, and fulfills the engagement criteria. Therefore, it acts as the **principal** and recognizes revenue on a **gross basis**.
    *   *SSP Partners:* For advertising sold through supply-side platform (SSP) partners, the text cuts off, but implies a different (likely net) treatment based on the principal/agent evaluation.

**Grok API Revenue Policy:**
*   Revenue from providing API access to Grok models is recognized **ratably over the contract term** (typically month-to-month or up to one year) for stand-ready access, or **as services are consumed** for usage-based arrangements.

## Implied Per-Grok-User Revenue Range and Monetization Density Analysis

### 1. Disclosed Inputs (from the SpaceX S-1)

| Metric | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Total MAUs (X + Grok), as of March 31, 2026 | 550 million | S-1 user metrics disclosure |
| Grok AI feature MAUs, as of March 31, 2026 | 117 million | S-1 user metrics disclosure |
| Total AI segment revenue, FY2025 | $3,201 million | S-1 segment financials |
| AI segment revenue composition: Advertising | $1,844 million (57.6%) | S-1 revenue disaggregation |
| AI segment revenue composition: AI Solutions & Infrastructure | $1,357 million (42.4%) | S-1 revenue disaggregation |
| Paid Grok subscribers (SuperGrok tiers), as of March 31, 2026 | ~1.9 million | S-1 subscriber metrics |
| Company-assumed weighted-avg. monthly Grok subscription revenue | $12/month ($144/year) | S-1 market opportunity section |

### 2. MAU Penetration Rate

Grok penetration = 117M / 550M = **21.27%**

This means approximately one in five MAUs across the combined X + Grok platform actively uses Grok's AI features.

### 3. Constructed Range of Implied Per-Grok-User Revenue

Using the total AI segment revenue of $3,201M and bracketing the denominator between (a) all 550M MAUs and (b) only the 117M Grok-feature MAUs:

| Bound | Calculation | Implied Per-User Revenue (Annual) |
|---|---|---|
| **Lower bound** (all MAUs as denominator) | $3,201M / 550M | **$5.82 per user** |
| **Upper bound** (Grok MAUs only as denominator) | $3,201M / 117M | **$27.36 per user** |

The range of **$5.82 – $27.36 per Grok user annually** reflects the uncertainty about how much of the AI segment's $3,201M is actually attributable to Grok-engaged users versus the broader X user base that generates advertising revenue.

### 4. Comparison to Standalone AI Subscription ARPU (OpenAI / ChatGPT)

Public reporting on OpenAI's ChatGPT subscription economics provides two reference ARPU anchors:

| Reference ARPU | Basis | Annual Value |
|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT Plus list price | $20/month × 12 | **$240 per subscriber/year** |
| OpenAI implied 2025 ARPU | $395 (2030 projected) / 1.38 (38% growth) | **~$286 per subscriber/year** |

**Grok's implied per-user monetization density vs. OpenAI ARPU:**

| Grok Bound | vs. ChatGPT Plus ($240) | vs. OpenAI 2025 ARPU ($286) |
|---|---|---|
| Lower ($5.82) | 2.4% | 2.0% |
| Upper ($27.36) | 11.4% | 9.6% |

**Interpretation:** Even at the most aggressive end of the range (assuming 100% of AI segment revenue is attributable to Grok's 117M MAUs), Grok's implied per-user monetization is only ~9–11% of OpenAI's standalone subscription ARPU. At the conservative end, it is ~2% of OpenAI's ARPU. This suggests Grok's current monetization density is **dramatically lower** than standalone AI subscription businesses on a per-engaged-user basis.

This gap is structurally explained by the S-1's own disclosures:
- **Only ~1.9 million of the 117M Grok MAUs are paid subscribers** (a paid penetration rate of just 1.62%), versus OpenAI's model where revenue is driven almost entirely by paying subscribers.
- The company itself assumes a weighted-average Grok subscription of only **$12/month ($144/year)** — well below ChatGPT Plus's $20/month — reflecting Grok's lower-income-country pricing strategy and bundled distribution through X.
- Grok's revenue mix is heavily weighted toward **advertising ($1,844M, 57.6% of AI segment revenue)**, which is monetized across the full X user base, not just Grok users. This dilutes any per-Grok-user revenue calculation that uses total AI segment revenue as the numerator.

In other words, Grok's monetization model is currently **advertising- and distribution-subsidized** rather than subscription-dense, which is the inverse of OpenAI's pure-subscription economics. The embedded X distribution allows Grok to reach 117M AI-feature users without requiring high per-user subscription revenue.

### 5. The Specific Disclosure Gap Preventing a Precise Comparison

The S-1 **does not isolate Grok's standalone subscription revenue** from the rest of the AI segment. Specifically:

1. **Grok subscription revenue is commingled with X subscription revenue.** The S-1 reports "X and Grok subscription revenue" as a single combined figure throughout MD&A (e.g., "an increase in X and Grok subscription revenue of $365 million"). There is no breakout of the Grok-only portion.

2. **Grok subscription revenue is further commingled with data licensing and API revenue** within the $1,357M "AI Solutions & Infrastructure" category. The S-1 groups three distinct streams — (i) X and Grok premium subscriptions, (ii) data licensing arrangements, and (iii) API access to Grok models — into one line item with no sub-disaggregation.

3. **Advertising revenue ($1,844M) is X-platform-centric, not Grok-centric.** The S-1 explicitly states advertising is generated from "ad products displayed on its X platform" and that the company does "not currently sell or offer advertisers the ability to place ads via the Grok API." Yet this X advertising revenue is included in the same AI segment total ($3,201M) used as the numerator, materially inflating any per-Grok-user calculation.

4. **Temporal mismatch.** The MAU figures (550M total; 117M Grok) are point-in-time as of **March 31, 2026**, while the $3,201M revenue figure covers **FY2025 (ended December 31, 2025)** — a three-month forward lag. User growth between those dates is not adjusted for in the revenue figure.

5. **MAUs ≠ paying subscribers.** The 117M Grok figure counts monthly active users of Grok's AI features (overwhelmingly free-tier), whereas OpenAI's ARPU benchmarks are calculated against **paying subscribers**. The S-1 discloses only ~1.9M paid Grok subscribers, meaning a like-for-like comparison would require dividing Grok-attributable subscription revenue by 1.9M — a figure the filing does not provide.

**Bottom line:** Because the S-1 does not separately disclose (a) Grok-only subscription revenue, (b) the split between X and Grok within the combined subscription line, or (c) the Grok-attributable portion of data licensing and API revenue, it is impossible to compute a precise Grok standalone ARPU comparable to OpenAI's. The $5.82–$27.36 range is therefore a wide bracket driven by denominator uncertainty, and the true Grok-specific per-user monetization figure lies somewhere within (or potentially below) this range — but cannot be pinpointed from the filing as disclosed.
